Fitting TF-IDF text vectorization on all clinical notes including test examples means that the term frequencies and inverse document frequencies used to represent clinical language incorporate information about the lexical distribution of test notes. For a multimodal diagnosis task where clinical notes are highly discriminative, this is a significant leakage vector. Additionally, image normalization using whole-set DICOM statistics (Hounsfield unit means and standard deviations computed over all images) means the image preprocessing is calibrated to the full dataset distribution rather than training data alone.

Second, the stratified random split across 24 months of patient episodes does not account for patient-level independence. If a patient had multiple episodes over 24 months (common in chronic disease populations), their earlier and later episodes could be distributed across training and test sets. The disease labels are derived from confirmed ICD-10 codes, so a patient's established diagnosis in a training episode would provide direct label signal for their test episode—a severe form of patient-level leakage in a diagnostic task.

Third, the fine-tuning of a ResNet-50 (ImageNet pretrained) on radiological images within the multimodal model creates a methodological asymmetry. The text-only baseline uses TF-IDF + XGBoost (no deep learning), while the multimodal model uses a CNN that learns end-to-end representations from radiological images. The performance difference between these models reflects both the informational advantage of imaging data and the representational advantage of deep learning over TF-IDF. A text-only deep learning baseline (e.g., a pretrained clinical BERT on notes) would be needed to isolate the contribution of the imaging modality.

Fourth, diagnostic labels derived from ICD-10 codes established post-hoc at clinical confirmation may introduce time-varying bias: patients with earlier disease confirmation (in the training period) may have more complete documentation than patients in the test period, creating documentation-level confounding that systematically differs between splits.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing leakage: TF-IDF vectorization and image normalization (DICOM Hounsfield statistics) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, encoding test-set lexical and imaging distributions into training preprocessing

Patient-level leakage: stratified random split at the episode level allows the same patients' multiple episodes to appear in both training and test, with established ICD-10 diagnoses from training episodes providing direct label signal for test episodes

Architecturally asymmetric comparison: text-only baseline uses shallow TF-IDF + XGBoost while multimodal model uses deep ResNet-50 fine-tuning; performance difference conflates modality advantage with deep learning representation advantage

ICD-10 label documentation bias: post-hoc diagnostic labels may be more completely documented for earlier training-period patients than later test-period patients, creating systematic documentation-level confounding across splits

No modality ablation or contribution analysis: the design reports only full multimodal vs. text-only comparison without ablating imaging-only, text-only deep learning, or structured-only models, leaving the contribution of each modality to multimodal performance entirely uncharacterized
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing leakage: TF-IDF vectorization and image normalization (DICOM Hounsfield statistics) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, encoding test-set lexical and imaging distributions into training preprocessing

Patient-level leakage: stratified random split at the episode level allows the same patients' multiple episodes to appear in both training and test, with established ICD-10 diagnoses from training episodes providing direct label signal for test episodes

Architecturally asymmetric comparison: text-only baseline uses shallow TF-IDF + XGBoost while multimodal model uses deep ResNet-50 fine-tuning; performance difference conflates modality advantage with deep learning representation advantage

ICD-10 label documentation bias: post-hoc diagnostic labels may be more completely documented for earlier training-period patients than later test-period patients, creating systematic documentation-level confounding across splits

No modality ablation or contribution analysis: the design reports only full multimodal vs. text-only comparison without ablating imaging-only, text-only deep learning, or structured-only models, leaving the contribution of each modality to multimodal performance entirely uncharacterized

critic_raw:
This experiment is methodologically stronger than many in this batch in one key respect: preprocessing is explicitly fit on the training set only, with imputation statistics, scaling parameters, TF-IDF vocabulary, and categorical encoders all derived from training data alone. This prevents the most common form of preprocessing leakage seen across other cases in this chunk.

However, the split strategy is a significant problem. The design uses stratified random sampling, holding out 15% of 100,000 records as the test set. For 36 months of manufacturing equipment sensor data at 1-minute intervals, random sampling of observation windows will place temporally adjacent and potentially overlapping windows in different splits. A rolling window of 100 consecutive 1-minute sensor readings (approximately 1.67 hours) means that any two windows separated by less than 100 minutes share overlapping sensor readings. Randomly distributing these autocorrelated windows across train and test creates autocorrelation leakage—test windows are near-identical to training windows in expectation, making the test evaluation measure memorization rather than generalization.

Second, the failure label is assigned based on whether 'unplanned maintenance occurred within 7 days of the observation timestamp.' With rolling windows computed at every minute, a failure event at time T will produce 10,080 positive-labeled windows spanning the 7-day window before T. These overlapping windows from the same failure event will be distributed across train and test by random sampling, meaning the model can learn from partial windows of a failure event in training and be evaluated on temporally adjacent windows from the same event in test.

Third, the operator log modality uses TF-IDF vectorization with 100 dimensions to encode semantic content of maintenance logs. For sparse, domain-specific technical text (maintenance action type, observed symptoms), TF-IDF at 100 dimensions may lose the categorical structure that would be better captured by direct categorical encoding with learned embeddings. The architecture also does not specify how operator log entries are aligned with sensor window timestamps when logs are sparse relative to 1-minute sensor readings.
all_issues_raised: Temporal autocorrelation leakage: random sampling of 100-minute rolling sensor windows from a continuous 36-month stream places overlapping windows in different splits, allowing test windows to be near-identical to training windows

Failure event window overlap across splits: a single failure event at time T generates 10,080 positive-labeled overlapping windows; random split distributes these across train and test, allowing the model to see partial windows of the same failure event during both training and evaluation

TF-IDF dimensionality mismatch for technical operator logs: 100-dimension TF-IDF is a poor representation for sparse domain-specific maintenance text that has strong categorical structure; direct categorical encoding with learned embeddings would be more appropriate

Operator log temporal alignment unspecified: the design does not address how operator logs (sparse, event-driven) are aligned with sensor windows (continuous, 1-minute) when logs are absent within a window—the 'missing token' strategy for absent logs conflates data absence with operational silence

No equipment-level independence in the split: if thousands of machines share the same facility infrastructure (shared coolant, power), inter-equipment correlations mean that randomly split windows from different machines may share common failure precursors that constitute leakage across equipment units
all_issues_adjudicated: Temporal autocorrelation leakage: random sampling of 100-minute rolling sensor windows from a continuous 36-month stream places overlapping windows in different splits, allowing test windows to be near-identical to training windows

Failure event window overlap across splits: a single failure event at time T generates 10,080 positive-labeled overlapping windows; random split distributes these across train and test, allowing the model to see partial windows of the same failure event during both training and evaluation

TF-IDF dimensionality mismatch for technical operator logs: 100-dimension TF-IDF is a poor representation for sparse domain-specific maintenance text that has strong categorical structure; direct categorical encoding with learned embeddings would be more appropriate

Operator log temporal alignment unspecified: the design does not address how operator logs (sparse, event-driven) are aligned with sensor windows (continuous, 1-minute) when logs are absent within a window—the 'missing token' strategy for absent logs conflates data absence with operational silence

No equipment-level independence in the split: if thousands of machines share the same facility infrastructure (shared coolant, power), inter-equipment correlations mean that randomly split windows from different machines may share common failure precursors that constitute leakage across equipment units

critic_raw:
This experiment has a well-structured temporal split that correctly mirrors deployment: training on earlier chronological data and evaluating on later data, with stratification by complication class within each split. The stated rationale—that temporal ordering prevents future clinical information from leaking into training—is sound, and the label definition (structured follow-up documentation independent of discharge note text) prevents the most obvious form of label leakage.

However, there is a clear preprocessing leakage problem. The design states: 'preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' For a transformer-based model where tokenization and vocabulary are critical for the text representation, fitting these on all 100,000 patient notes—including the 20,000 in the test set—means the vocabulary incorporates lexical information from test documents. While standard transformer tokenizers use fixed pretrained vocabularies (e.g., BPE for BERT/GPT), any custom vocabulary or text normalization statistics derived from the full dataset constitute leakage. The design does not clarify whether a fixed pretrained tokenizer or a custom-fit one is used.

Second, the multi-class complication prediction task is framed as assigning 'the primary or most severe complication' as a single class label. This label construction requires post-discharge follow-up documentation and readmission records over 30 days. If the clinical notes used as model inputs include any documentation that temporally overlaps with or references the post-discharge period, there is a risk that the input features encode information about the complication outcome. The design addresses this by specifying that labels come from structured follow-up data 'not inferred from discharge summary text'—which is appropriate—but does not confirm that no post-discharge documentation is included in the clinical notes used as input features.

Third, the experiment does not address class imbalance in the multi-class complication target. With 'none' (no complication) likely dominating the 30-day window for most patients, and individual complication types being rare, standard cross-entropy training will be strongly biased toward predicting 'no complication.' No class weighting, focal loss, or oversampling strategy is specified.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing leakage risk: tokenizer, text normalizer, and vocabulary builder are stated as fit on the full dataset before splitting; if any custom vocabulary statistics are derived from all 100,000 notes, test-set lexical information contaminates the training pipeline

Post-discharge note contamination risk: the design does not confirm that clinical notes used as input features are restricted to pre-discharge documentation, leaving open the possibility that post-discharge follow-up notes are inadvertently included as inputs

Multi-class imbalance unaddressed: the 'none' class (no complication) dominates 30-day post-discharge outcomes; no class weighting, focal loss, or resampling strategy is specified to prevent the model from defaulting to predicting no complication for all patients

Single-complication label per patient loses clinical complexity: assigning the 'primary or most severe complication' as a single label ignores comorbid complications and creates an arbitrary label construction that may not align with clinical outcomes or patient burden

No confidence interval or statistical significance testing: the evaluation does not specify how performance differences between transformer and logistic regression will be tested for statistical significance (e.g., bootstrap CI, McNemar's test), leaving no principled way to distinguish real improvements from sampling noise
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing leakage risk: tokenizer, text normalizer, and vocabulary builder are stated as fit on the full dataset before splitting; if any custom vocabulary statistics are derived from all 100,000 notes, test-set lexical information contaminates the training pipeline

Post-discharge note contamination risk: the design does not confirm that clinical notes used as input features are restricted to pre-discharge documentation, leaving open the possibility that post-discharge follow-up notes are inadvertently included as inputs

Multi-class imbalance unaddressed: the 'none' class (no complication) dominates 30-day post-discharge outcomes; no class weighting, focal loss, or resampling strategy is specified to prevent the model from defaulting to predicting no complication for all patients

Single-complication label per patient loses clinical complexity: assigning the 'primary or most severe complication' as a single label ignores comorbid complications and creates an arbitrary label construction that may not align with clinical outcomes or patient burden

No confidence interval or statistical significance testing: the evaluation does not specify how performance differences between transformer and logistic regression will be tested for statistical significance (e.g., bootstrap CI, McNemar's test), leaving no principled way to distinguish real improvements from sampling noise
